So people who believe they must be instantly, constantly, or quickly accessible view themselves as providing service. That's not our role. Our role is taking control of getting compliance from getting results for. Our role is not providing service. The easy, instant, quick access is service role. And so this is a role issue. Last, easy and random access creates an expectation that can never consistently be met. So if you put it into a relationship, you are guaranteeing eventually you are going to have annoyed, frustrated, pissed off people and you're going to lose them. I'd rather have annoyed, frustrated, pissed off at the beginning and deal with it then than deal with it later. It's an expectation that cannot be met. It can if you have one client. Maybe if you have three, but pretty much as soon as you get to like five or ten or fifteen, what happens if all five of them want to talk to you at the same time? You can't meet the expectation. So if you want to manage expectations, don't be creating expectations you can't meet. That's far worse than convincing them to accept a situation they don't like to start with. Far worse. Other access. Dropping by. They just going to drop by. I hope not. Unless you're running a coffee shop. There you want them dropping by. Our businesses we don't want them dropping by. I get it. We get it from people until they're educated. Oh, the next time I'm in Phoenix, I'll drop by. Go ahead, because first of all I'm never there. Pretty much Vicki's not there much. If you want to drop by, drop by. You can look in the building. Even when I was there, it was like you're going to do what? No, no, no. No, you're not. Drop by. I got plans. You don't want people dropping by. I never received them when they did. Even if I wanted to. Bad idea. Bad precedent. Because first of all, again, you set up an expectation now you can't consistently meet. They tell, remember, you create a reputation in part because of what they tell other people. So if they tell other people, they can drop by and see you. Other people want to drop by and see you. Pretty soon, everybody's dropping by to see you. You can't see everybody. You've created an expectation. You can't meet. I'll just call up when I'm in town and we'll go to dinner. It's okay if you only know one person who ever comes to town. It's no good if you know 20 because they tell each other. So now your reputation is somebody who at the drop of a hat will go to dinner. Now when you can't, you got really unhappy people. It's really bad positioning. What important person do you know who you can actually just drop by and have them drop everything they're doing and go to dinner? No important person functions this way. Only unimportant people function this way. Billionaires don't do it to each other. Warren Buffett just does it land in Seattle on a whim and call up dates and say, let's go play canasta. I'll be over in about an hour. That's not what they do to each other because probably Gates is doing something already. He's like busy making money, spending money, doing saving the orphans, whatever the hell he's doing now, but he's like doing something. They like playing. So how you handle this conveys a lot to people. It or delayed response, same thing. If you think you are in the servant role, we're going to break it in five minutes. If you think you are in the servant role, then you will want to respond to everybody quickly. From a client control standpoint, it's the worst thing you'll ever do to yourself because you'll set up an expectation you can't possibly meet on a continuous basis.
